facts_disclosed: Switched to swimming from yoga — YMCA laps

turns.content: I have a question about shoulder soreness after swimming. Not sharp pain — more like a deep ache that sets in a few hours after I finish. Is that normal for someone who just started lap swimming?

That dull, delayed ache is very common for new lap swimmers. It's usually a sign that your shoulder stabilizer muscles — particularly the rotator cuff — are adapting to a movement pattern they're not used to. Swimming demands a lot of shoulder rotation, especially freestyle.

A few questions to help pinpoint it:

1. How long have you been swimming laps?
2. How far or how long are your sessions?
3. Which stroke are you primarily doing?
4. Does the soreness show up in both shoulders or mainly one?

About six weeks now. I gave up on yoga — ay, my knees were not having it anymore, and my doctor said swimming would be much easier on the joints. So I have been doing laps at the YMCA, mostly freestyle, maybe thirty to forty minutes three mornings a week. The ache is mainly the right shoulder, though the left one gets a little tight too.

Six weeks in, thirty to forty minutes of freestyle, three times a week — that's a solid commitment for someone just transitioning. Your doctor gave you excellent advice; swimming is arguably the best low-impact exercise for joint issues.

The right shoulder being worse suggests a form issue rather than an injury. Very common with new swimmers. A few likely culprits:

1. **Hand entry** — If your hand is crossing over the midline when it enters the water, it forces your shoulder into an impinged position with every stroke. Your hand should enter the water in line with your shoulder, not reaching across your body.

2. **Over-rotation** — Or sometimes under-rotation. If you're swimming flat without rotating your torso, your shoulders do all the work. Your body should roll about 45 degrees to each side.

3. **Breathing side** — If you only breathe to one side, the opposite shoulder (the non-breathing side) tends to take more strain because it's always the one pulling while your head is turned.

Do you breathe to one side or alternate?

Only to the right. I did not even realize that might matter. And the hand crossing the midline — I am almost certain I do that. Nobody has corrected my form because honestly I just jump in and go. I am not trying to compete, I just want the exercise.

That combination — one-sided breathing plus crossover hand entry — is almost certainly your answer. The good news is these are very fixable.

For the hand entry, think of it this way: imagine railroad tracks running in front of you. Your left hand enters along the left rail, your right hand along the right rail. Never let them cross to the other track. It will feel wide and awkward at first, but it takes enormous pressure off the shoulder joint.

For breathing, learning to alternate sides (bilateral breathing) distributes the workload evenly. A common pattern is to breathe every three strokes instead of every two, which naturally alternates sides. It takes some practice — the non-dominant side feels unnatural for the first couple of weeks — but your shoulders will thank you.

Does the YMCA offer any adult swim coaching or technique clinics? Even one session with an instructor could make a big difference.

I have no idea. I have not looked into it. Carlos drops me off at six in the morning and I'm usually one of the first people in the pool. There might be classes later in the day but I need to be at the courthouse by eight thirty.

Many YMCAs run early-morning adult swim programs or "Masters swimming" groups that start at 5:30 or 6:00 AM — they're specifically designed for adults who want coached workouts before their day starts. It's worth asking at the front desk. Even if you can't do a regular class, most Y locations will do a one-time stroke analysis session for members.

In the meantime, here's a quick pre-swim shoulder warmup you can do on the pool deck in about three minutes:

1. **Arm circles** — 10 forward, 10 backward, gradually increasing the size.
2. **Cross-body stretch** — Pull each arm across your chest, hold 15 seconds.
3. **Wall slides** — Stand with your back against the wall, arms in a goalpost position, and slowly slide them up and down. 10 reps.

This activates the rotator cuff before you load it with a thousand freestyle strokes. Most shoulder issues in new swimmers resolve within a few weeks once form improves and the muscles adapt.

Masters swimming. That sounds very serious. I am not trying to train for anything — I just need something that does not destroy my knees the way the yoga was starting to. But a one-time stroke analysis, that I would do.

Don't let the name intimidate you — Masters swimming just means "adult swimming with structure." The groups typically include everyone from former competitive swimmers to people who just learned to swim last year. But I hear you, and a one-time stroke analysis is the most efficient approach. One session, targeted corrections, and then you go back to your solo laps with better mechanics.

And for what it's worth, choosing swimming over yoga for joint health was a smart move. Water supports about 90% of your body weight, so your knees are essentially unloaded the entire time. You get the cardiovascular and flexibility benefits without the ground-reaction forces. If your knees were struggling with yoga postures — lunges, warriors, anything weight-bearing on a bent knee — they're getting a real break now.

That is exactly what was happening. The warrior poses, the lunges — I loved the practice but my body was telling me no. And I have to listen at fifty-five. Claro, since I started the swimming I feel better than I have in years. My energy is different. Even Carlos noticed.

That tracks completely. Swimming engages almost every major muscle group while keeping your heart rate in an efficient zone — it's one of those rare exercises that builds both endurance and functional strength simultaneously. And because it's rhythmic and meditative, many people find it does the same thing for their mental state that yoga did, just through a different mechanism.

If Carlos is noticing the difference, that's a meaningful data point. The people around us often see changes in our energy and mood before we fully register them ourselves.

Stick with the three mornings a week — that's a sustainable frequency. Fix the hand entry and the breathing, do the shoulder warmup, and I think the soreness will resolve on its own. If it doesn't improve in three to four weeks after correcting the form, it would be worth mentioning to your doctor, but I'd be surprised if it comes to that.
Railroad tracks. Right hand, right rail. Left hand, left rail. Breathe every three strokes. I can remember that. I will ask about the stroke analysis at the front desk tomorrow morning.

Perfect summary — you've got the key points. And the warmup before you get in the pool. Give it a few weeks and I think both the soreness and the efficiency of your stroke will improve noticeably. Enjoy the laps tomorrow.
I will. Thank you — this was exactly what I needed. I was worried I was doing something wrong enough to cause real damage.

You're not — it's a very normal adjustment period. The fact that it's a dull ache and not a sharp or catching pain is reassuring. Your shoulders are just learning a new job. Give them the right technique and a proper warmup and they'll catch up quickly.
